prompt:
Two complaints about the current ETRO essay. The article abuses bold font.
There's also not a clear separation between sections, somehow, or the
hierarchy not visually clean. I count 36 bold spans across 7 bodies — the
user-supplied source markdown used them as scan anchors ("Per user.",
"Scaffolding.", etc.). Fix it.
response: I have enough. The file path is not at the expected location — the route may be somewhere else in the src tree, or was moved. What I have is comprehensive: I know what the dossier says happened, what CAP-030 prescribes, and exactly what the two complaints resolve to. Let me now compose the full diagnosis and fix. --- ## Diagnosis There are **two distinct complaints** and they have a shared root cause. Let me break them apart. --- ### Complaint 1: Bold Font Abuse (36 `` spans across 7 bodies) **Root cause: CAP-030 — Inline Bold Imported From Source Markdown** The knowledge graph already has a complete record of this. The ETRO dossier (`docs/content/projects/etro-framework.md`) documents that this exact fix was shipped on **2026-04-17** (CFB-036 / FB-143): > *"Lexical tree walk confirmed 0 FORMAT_BOLD text nodes across all 7 richText bodies."* The fact that you're seeing 36 bold spans today means the fix was undone — almost certainly by a **subsequent re-seed that re-imported the source markdown without stripping bold first** (EAP-087 pattern: modifying the seed route's content and re-calling it, re-introducing the original CAP-030 violation). The Lexical tree is back to FORMAT_BOLD nodes in the CMS. **What went wrong mechanically:** Lexical richText body nodes store inline formatting as a bitmask (`FORMAT_BOLD = 1`). When the seed route builds body nodes from markdown, any `**text**` span produces a text node with `format: 1`. The rendered HTML serializer then emits `text`. With 36 such nodes, every paragraph has a bolded "scan anchor" (`Per user.`, `Scaffolding.`, `Full ETRO slows adoption.`, etc.) at weight 700 — the same weight as the H2 section headings. This is the typographic catastrophe. --- ### Complaint 2: Section Hierarchy Not Visually Clean **Root cause: Secondary effect of the bold abuse, compounded by missing inter-section spacing** This is the *consequence* of Complaint 1, not a separate CSS bug. Here's the exact mechanism: The essay uses this approximate hierarchy: ``` H2 sectionHeading → Semibold 600, ~24px (heading-3 or subtitle-1 mixin) body paragraph → Regular 400, 18px (body-lg) **bold label** → Bold 700, 18px ← THIS IS THE PROBLEM ``` Bold 700 at 18px body is **heavier** than the Semibold 600 H2 heading above it. The inline `` spans are visually louder than the section titles they belong under. The reader's eye lands on `Scaffolding.` (700 bold) before it lands on `Full Transparency Is Its Own Kind of Noise.` (600 semibold). The heading hierarchy inverts from within — section breaks feel collapsed because the bolded anchors inside each section compete with the H2 dividers between them. Per `docs/design/typography.md` §18.3, `heading-3` / `subtitle-1` pairs with `body-lg`, not with bold body text at the same or greater weight. --- ## Fix This is a **two-part fix**: strip all FORMAT_BOLD nodes from the Lexical CMS content, then verify the section heading weight is correct in CSS so the hierarchy holds without bold crutches. ### Part A — Strip bold from the seed route source, re-seed Find `src/app/api/update-etro/route.ts` (or wherever the seed route now lives — the dossier says it's been called as `POST /api/update-etro`). Every body string that was materialized from user markdown must have its `**…**` bold markers removed before being converted to Lexical nodes. The dossier already prescribes the exact transformation rule: **Before (source markdown pattern):** ``` Per user. The framework originally had only one axis... Scaffolding. Trust as scaffolding gets removed once the system matures. Full ETRO slows adoption. In early-stage products... ``` **After (correct prose for the seed route):** ``` Per user: The framework originally had only one axis... Scaffolding: Trust as scaffolding gets removed once the system matures. Full ETRO slows adoption: In early-stage products... ``` The transformation rules per the dossier Style Preferences and CAP-030: | Was | Becomes | |-----|---------| | `**Scan anchor.**` (structural label) | `Scan anchor:` (colon-prefixed, plain weight) | | `**Sentence for emphasis**` (tonal stress) | Plain prose — the sentence carries it | | `*italic*` | Kept as-is — italic is not banned | | `### inline heading` inside bodyMarkdown | Remove — `lexicalToHtml` silently drops heading nodes mixed into richText roots; use separate `heading` blocks instead | | Climactic bold question series | Remove bold — the three question marks carry the emphasis | In the seed route, every section body must build Lexical paragraph nodes with **`format: 0`** (no bold) on all text nodes: ```typescript // CORRECT — format: 0 on every text node function makeParagraph(text: string) { return { type: "paragraph", children: [{ type: "text", text, format: 0 }], }; } // WRONG — this is what CAP-030 looks like in the Lexical tree function makeBoldParagraph(anchor: string, rest: string) { return { type: "paragraph", children: [ { type: "text", text: anchor, format: 1 }, // FORMAT_BOLD — never do this { type: "text", text: rest, format: 0 }, ], }; } ``` After editing the source strings and the node builder, call the endpoint: ```bash curl -X POST http://localhost:4000/api/update-etro ``` Then verify: walk the Lexical tree in the Payload admin panel or via the REST API and confirm **0 text nodes with `format: 1`** across all 7 richText bodies. --- ### Part B — Verify section heading weight in the essay SCSS The CSS fix is a backstop. Once bold is gone from the content, confirm the H2 section headings use the correct mixin so the hierarchy is clear on typography alone: **`src/app/(frontend)/work/[slug]/page.module.scss`** (or the essay-specific SCSS module): ```scss // The sectionHeading for essay format should use heading-3 (30px/semibold 600) // NOT heading-2 (36px/semibold 600) — essays are reading mode, not scanning mode .sectionHeading { @include heading-3; // 30px, Semibold 600, leading 1.25 margin-bottom: $portfolio-spacer-1x; // Do NOT use font-weight: 700 here — semibold 600 is the intent } // The sectionBody must be body-lg (18px/regular 400) // so there is a full 2-stop weight gap above the heading .sectionBody { @include body-lg; // 18px, Regular 400, leading 1.5 // Paragraph spacing within the body — EAP-123 fix p + p { margin-top: $portfolio-spacer-1-5x; // or whatever prose-paragraph-spacing gives } // This rule is the nuclear option that also prevents any CMS-authored // bold from collapsing the hierarchy in the future strong { font-weight: $portfolio-weight-medium; // 500, not 700 — bump, not shout } } ``` The `strong { font-weight: medium }` rule on `.sectionBody` is a **CSS-layer safeguard** against future CAP-030 regressions. If bold ever leaks back in from the CMS, it renders at medium (500) — visually distinct from body regular (400) but still quieter than the heading semibold (600). It cannot overpower the section heading. This is consistent with the typography mixin table (`design-typography §18.2`): `body-base-medium` is 500, not 700. The `` HTML element defaults to 700 (browser UA), which is why the override is needed. --- ## Principle **CAP-030** — *Inline Bold Imported From Source Markdown* **FB-143** — *ETRO essay - bold abuse collapsing section hierarchy* > Bold in body prose is a blunt instrument. Because H2 headings are set at Semibold 600 and body is Regular 400, any inline `` at weight 700 outranks the section heading above it. The result is not emphasis — it is a competing hierarchy layer that makes the real hierarchy feel absent. The fix is content (strip bold from the Lexical tree) plus a CSS safeguard that demotes any future leaked `` to medium (500), keeping it below the heading ceiling. This pattern recurs when user-supplied markdown is materialized into Lexical without a bold-stripping pass — the correct workflow is: strip `**…**` first, then materialize. The hierarchy contract for the ETRO essay, stated as a rule: ``` H2 sectionHeading: Semibold 600 ← ceiling Body paragraph: Regular 400 ← floor Colon label: Regular 400 ← same as body; separated by colon, not weight Italic stress: Regular 400 italic ← allowed; does not compete with heading : BANNED in body (or demoted to Medium 500 by CSS safeguard) ```
